questions.prompt: The critic's [[1]] of the novel's reputation is not mere [[2]]; she marshals textual evidence so precisely that even the author's most ardent defenders have found her central claims difficult to [[3]].
questions.explanation: Dismantling a reputation = taking it apart. The critic's contrarianism (going against received opinion) is distinguished from mere contrarianism (empty opposition) by the marshaling of evidence. The claims are difficult to contest = hard to argue against.

questions.prompt: Despite the organization's [[1]] rhetoric about transparency, its actual disclosure practices were notable primarily for what they [[2]]: key decisions were announced long after the relevant window for public input had closed.
questions.explanation: Rhetoric vs. practice = performative (theatrical, for show). Announcements came too late for input = key decisions were concealed until the window closed.

questions.prompt: The novelist's refusal to provide stable points of narrative orientation is not a failure of craft but a deliberate [[1]]: it reproduces in the reader the disorientation that is the novel's actual subject.
questions.explanation: Deliberate and purposeful = a technique (a crafted method). Not an affectation (pretentious mannerism) or contrivance (an artificial device) — it serves a specific mimetic purpose.

questions.prompt: The court's ruling was [[1]] in its practical effect even if technically [[2]]: by refusing to decide the constitutional question, the justices left in place a lower court decision whose [[3]] reach extended to every state in the circuit.
questions.explanation: Refusing to decide the constitutional question is technically sound (procedurally correct), but its effect is sweeping: leaving the lower court's expansive decision in place affected every state in the circuit.

questions.prompt: The general's memoirs are a [[1]] document: they illuminate certain operational decisions with remarkable clarity while remaining resolutely [[2]] about the political pressures that shaped those decisions from above.
questions.explanation: Revealing about some things, opaque (deliberately unclear) about others — the memoirist chooses what to illuminate and what to hide. Tendentious would mean biased, which the contrast doesn't set up.

questions.prompt: The administration's energy policy was criticized for being [[1]] — addressing short-term electoral pressures at the expense of the structural reforms that the crisis actually demanded.
questions.explanation: Addressing short-term electoral pressures at the expense of structural reform = sacrificing principle for convenience. Expedient and opportunistic both describe this self-interested short-termism.

questions.prompt: The documentary's framing of its subject was so [[1]] that viewers with no prior knowledge of the controversy left with a clear sense of who the heroes and villains were.
questions.explanation: Framing that leaves viewers with predetermined moral judgments = tendentious (biased toward a conclusion) and slanted (angled toward one side).

questions.prompt: The composer's late works achieve their effects through [[1]] rather than elaboration: a single melodic phrase, repeated with minute variations, accrues an emotional weight that conventionally structured music rarely attains.
questions.explanation: A single phrase repeated with variations that builds emotional weight over time = accretion and accumulation, both meaning gradual growth by addition.

questions.prompt: The delegation's decision to walk out rather than negotiate was [[1]] — it foreclosed the possibility of any agreement at a moment when a deal was more achievable than it had been in years.
questions.explanation: Foreclosing agreement at the most promising moment = failing to see consequences (shortsighted) and producing the opposite of the intended result (counterproductive).

questions.prompt: The political scientist's model is [[1]] in its assumptions, requiring that voters process information and weigh alternatives in ways that decades of behavioral research have shown to be rare even among highly engaged citizens.
questions.explanation: Requiring behaviors that are behaviorally unrealistic = assuming an ideal (idealized) or exceptional (heroic) voter that doesn't exist in practice.

questions.passage:
Legal positivism holds that the existence and content of law depends on social facts — specifically, on the enactments and practices of legal institutions — rather than on its merits. On this view, a law can be valid even if it is unjust, and what the law is can always be determined independently of what the law ought to be. H.L.A. Hart's version of positivism refined this claim by distinguishing between primary rules (rules of obligation that govern conduct) and secondary rules (rules about rules — those that specify how primary rules are created, changed, and adjudicated). The union of primary and secondary rules, Hart argued, is the key to understanding a legal system as a system rather than as a mere collection of disconnected obligations.

Ronald Dworkin's attack on positivism focused on what he called the 'model of rules' — the positivist assumption that law consists entirely of rules. Legal practice, Dworkin argued, also includes principles, which differ from rules in that they do not apply in an all-or-nothing fashion but instead have weight that judges must balance against competing considerations. Because principles cannot be identified by any master rule (Hart's 'rule of recognition'), they undermine the positivist claim that all law derives its validity from social facts alone.
questions.prompt: The primary purpose of the passage is to
options: argue that Dworkin's account of legal principles is more accurate than Hart's positivism
present a theoretical position in legal philosophy and an influential critique of it
demonstrate that legal positivism cannot account for judicial practice in any legal system
defend Hart's distinction between primary and secondary rules against Dworkin's objections
show that law and morality are necessarily connected through the role of principles
questions.answer: present a theoretical position in legal philosophy and an influential critique of it
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 presents Hart's legal positivism; paragraph 2 presents Dworkin's critique. The author presents both without endorsing either.

questions.passage:
Legal positivism holds that the existence and content of law depends on social facts — specifically, on the enactments and practices of legal institutions — rather than on its merits. On this view, a law can be valid even if it is unjust, and what the law is can always be determined independently of what the law ought to be. H.L.A. Hart's version of positivism refined this claim by distinguishing between primary rules (rules of obligation that govern conduct) and secondary rules (rules about rules — those that specify how primary rules are created, changed, and adjudicated). The union of primary and secondary rules, Hart argued, is the key to understanding a legal system as a system rather than as a mere collection of disconnected obligations.

Ronald Dworkin's attack on positivism focused on what he called the 'model of rules' — the positivist assumption that law consists entirely of rules. Legal practice, Dworkin argued, also includes principles, which differ from rules in that they do not apply in an all-or-nothing fashion but instead have weight that judges must balance against competing considerations. Because principles cannot be identified by any master rule (Hart's 'rule of recognition'), they undermine the positivist claim that all law derives its validity from social facts alone.
questions.prompt: According to the passage, Dworkin's critique targets the positivist claim that
options: judges should apply rules rather than exercise discretion
legal systems can be distinguished from collections of disconnected obligations
law is valid only when it is consistent with moral principles
all legally valid norms can be identified through social facts
primary and secondary rules together constitute the foundation of law
questions.answer: all legally valid norms can be identified through social facts
questions.explanation: Dworkin argues that principles cannot be identified by any master rule (a social fact), so not all legally operative norms are grounded in social facts — directly targeting the positivist claim.

questions.passage:
Legal positivism holds that the existence and content of law depends on social facts — specifically, on the enactments and practices of legal institutions — rather than on its merits. On this view, a law can be valid even if it is unjust, and what the law is can always be determined independently of what the law ought to be. H.L.A. Hart's version of positivism refined this claim by distinguishing between primary rules (rules of obligation that govern conduct) and secondary rules (rules about rules — those that specify how primary rules are created, changed, and adjudicated). The union of primary and secondary rules, Hart argued, is the key to understanding a legal system as a system rather than as a mere collection of disconnected obligations.

Ronald Dworkin's attack on positivism focused on what he called the 'model of rules' — the positivist assumption that law consists entirely of rules. Legal practice, Dworkin argued, also includes principles, which differ from rules in that they do not apply in an all-or-nothing fashion but instead have weight that judges must balance against competing considerations. Because principles cannot be identified by any master rule (Hart's 'rule of recognition'), they undermine the positivist claim that all law derives its validity from social facts alone.
questions.prompt: According to the passage, legal principles differ from legal rules in that principles
options: are created by legislation rather than by judicial precedent
apply only to cases involving constitutional questions
do not apply mechanically but must be weighed against other considerations
are not subject to the secondary rules that govern rule change
cannot be used to resolve conflicts between competing primary rules
questions.answer: do not apply mechanically but must be weighed against other considerations
questions.explanation: The passage states explicitly: principles 'do not apply in an all-or-nothing fashion but instead have weight that judges must balance against competing considerations.'

questions.passage:
The debate over whether consciousness can be explained in purely physical terms — the 'hard problem' of consciousness as David Chalmers named it — has generated more philosophical heat than scientific light. Critics of physicalism point to the 'explanatory gap': even a complete physical description of the brain would leave open the question of why any of that processing is accompanied by subjective experience at all. Physicalists have responded in two main ways. Eliminativists argue that phenomenal consciousness, as ordinarily conceived, is a theoretical posit that will eventually be replaced by a better neuroscientific vocabulary, much as 'heat' was replaced by mean molecular kinetic energy. Illusionists take the more deflationary view that conscious experience seems to have properties it does not actually have, and that explaining why it seems that way is all that remains to be explained.

Neither response has satisfied critics who regard the existence of subjective experience as too obvious to deny and too fundamental to dissolve into neuroscience. The impasse suggests that the debate may be partly terminological — turning on what exactly is meant by 'explanation' and whether the standards applicable to other domains of inquiry are applicable here.
questions.prompt: The author suggests that the impasse in the debate over consciousness may result from
options: the failure of physicalists to conduct adequate empirical research
unresolved disagreement about what would count as an explanation
the technical limitations of current neuroscientific methods
the personal and emotional investment of participants in the debate
eliminativism's inability to account for well-established neuroscientific findings
questions.answer: unresolved disagreement about what would count as an explanation
questions.explanation: 'The impasse suggests the debate may be partly terminological — turning on what exactly is meant by explanation.' That is unresolved disagreement about the standard of explanation.

questions.passage:
The debate over whether consciousness can be explained in purely physical terms — the 'hard problem' of consciousness as David Chalmers named it — has generated more philosophical heat than scientific light. Critics of physicalism point to the 'explanatory gap': even a complete physical description of the brain would leave open the question of why any of that processing is accompanied by subjective experience at all. Physicalists have responded in two main ways. Eliminativists argue that phenomenal consciousness, as ordinarily conceived, is a theoretical posit that will eventually be replaced by a better neuroscientific vocabulary, much as 'heat' was replaced by mean molecular kinetic energy. Illusionists take the more deflationary view that conscious experience seems to have properties it does not actually have, and that explaining why it seems that way is all that remains to be explained.

Neither response has satisfied critics who regard the existence of subjective experience as too obvious to deny and too fundamental to dissolve into neuroscience. The impasse suggests that the debate may be partly terminological — turning on what exactly is meant by 'explanation' and whether the standards applicable to other domains of inquiry are applicable here.
questions.prompt: The analogy between 'heat' and phenomenal consciousness is invoked by eliminativists to suggest that
options: heat and consciousness are both manifestations of the same underlying physical process
phenomenal consciousness is a pre-scientific concept that science will eventually supersede
the hard problem of consciousness has already been solved in principle by physicalism
ordinary language is an unreliable guide to the nature of subjective experience
the neuroscientific vocabulary needed to replace phenomenal concepts has already been developed
questions.answer: phenomenal consciousness is a pre-scientific concept that science will eventually supersede
questions.explanation: 'Heat' was eventually replaced by mean molecular kinetic energy — a pre-scientific folk concept superseded by science. Eliminativists invoke this to argue the same will happen to 'phenomenal consciousness.'
